project_overview.project_name: Support for Social Recovery Needs of Vulnerable Groups Phase II
project_overview.project_development_objective: To expand support for the immediate social recovery needs of vulnerable groups in Lebanon
project_overview.beneficiaries: 15,475 beneficiaries including: 3,500 GBV and child beneficiaries, 8,334 mental health beneficiaries, and 3,641 older persons and persons with disabilities. Forcibly displaced persons (FDPs) and migrants are targeted across all workstreams

geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - Lebanon is classified as a fragile state. The country has experienced multiple concurrent crises since 2019 including financial crisis, Port of Beirut explosion (August 2020), COVID-19 pandemic, Russia-Ukraine conflict spillover effects, and regional tensions since October 2023 with limited escalation of conflict in Southern Lebanon

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Support for Social Services for Vulnerable Groups
key_components.description: Finances NGOs/CSOs to provide social services through three sub-components: (1.1) Enhanced Support for Survivors and those at-risk of GBV & Children - operating safe shelters/spaces, case management, psychosocial support, legal assistance, economic empowerment programs, and child protection services; (1.2) Enhanced Support for Psychosocial Wellbeing - sustaining national mental health hotline (Lifeline), Step-by-Step digital platform, Self Help Plus stress management course, and mental health in workplace programs; (1.3) Enhanced Support for Older Persons and Persons with Disabilities - Community-Based Inclusive Development services, specialized health services via PHCCs and MMUs, and digital CBR portal

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Sub-grantee Capacity Building for Impact & Sustainability
key_components.description: Provides intensive capacity building support to NGO sub-grantees through IRC's Partnership Excellence for Equality and Results System (PEERS). Includes capacity assessments, tailored capacity building plans, and hands-on support in project management, financial management, procurement, environmental & social safeguards, and institutional governance. Introduces a 'graduation process' for NGO partners to enhance their readiness to attract additional donor resources

Finances project operating and management costs including consultancy fees and administrative costs for overall project management, fiduciary and environmental/social standards management, capacity building oversight, and monitoring and evaluation

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: International Rescue Committee Inc (IRC) - both grant recipient and implementing agency. IRC Lebanon is a branch office of IRC Inc (non-profit corporation organized under New York law). IRC Inc signs the Grant Agreement and remains responsible for implementation, while IRC Lebanon serves as country-level focal point
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Multi-stakeholder partnership approach including: coordination with PROSPECTS partners (UNICEF, UNHCR, ILO) and Government of Netherlands; partnership with 10 national NGOs/CSOs receiving sub-grants; technical partnerships with Ministry of Public Health-National Mental Health Programme (MoPH-NMHP), Ministry of Justice (MoJ), and Ministry of Social Affairs (MoSA); participation in 10 programmatic working groups at national/local level; partnerships with 29 local organizations, 3 primary healthcare centers, and 2 private sector entities
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Framework (July 2016, revised November 2020). Sub-grant selection via three pathways: open competitive selection, limited competitive selection, and direct selection. Multi-leveled due diligence approach including compliant selection process, enhanced vetting (Partner Identity and Background Review including anti-terrorism compliance checks), and sub-grantee institutional/capacity review. Systematic Tracking of Exchanges in Procurement (STEP) system used. Project Procurement Strategy for Development (PPSD) to broaden social and employment benefits
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: IRC applies robust Monitoring, Evaluation, Accountability and Learning (MEAL) with Senior MEAL Officer providing support. Bi-annual narrative progress reports tracking implementation plan and results framework. Baseline data and targets verified within 6 months of implementation. Sub-grantee partners assessed for M&E and compliance mechanisms with capacity-building plans developed. Data collection tools developed for results framework indicators. Beneficiary feedback collected via surveys, focus group discussions, and key informant interviews. Quarterly reporting from implementing partners consolidated by IRC. Mid-Term Review mission planned for September/October 2025

identified_risks.risk_description: Lebanon's severe economic crisis with currency depreciation (LBP devalued >90%), triple-digit inflation, balance of payments deficit, and shrinking employment sector. Fifth year of economic contraction as of 2023 with cumulative 37.2% contraction between 2018-2021. Spillover effects from Middle East conflict further diminish stabilization prospects
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Preserve purchasing power of project funds by maximizing US dollar value; use Designated Account in USD at JP Morgan Chase; regular review of benefit amounts in LBP; maintain close coordination with World Bank Task Team

identified_risks.risk_description: Complex nature of grants and procurement of services; high perception of fraud and corruption in country; high staff turnover in public/private companies; spread of activities across multiple NGOs increases risk of misuse of funds; deterioration of local currency value
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: IRC has gained experience executing Bank-financed projects with dedicated team; use of USD Designated Account and Operating Account to preserve fund value; implementation of detailed arrangements and monitoring controls for NGO recruitment as agreed in POM; external audit by independent auditor acceptable to World Bank; robust control arrangements including separate bank accounts for each NGO, tranche-based payments, quarterly reporting with supporting documentation, and IRC review and clearance before next advance

identified_risks.risk_description: 13-month political deadlock following Port of Beirut explosion, caretaker government since September 2021, parliament unable to elect President despite repeated attempts, recurrent social tensions and low trust between citizens and state
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Not explicitly detailed but project design works through non-governmental sector given weak government systems; World Bank's ability to convene stakeholders; IRC's experience in FCV contexts

identified_risks.risk_description: Regional tensions since October 2023 have caused limited escalation of conflict in Southern Lebanon. Broader escalation of tensions and/or conflict events in region could rapidly and unpredictably augment national context
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: IRC is well-accustomed to working under emergency and FCV conditions with sufficient global and country capacity and experience to ensure operations could continue under such conditions in a risk-appropriate manner

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: Government systems remain weak and inadequate for meeting needs; limited state resources and institutional capacity; many local NGOs lack capacity to meet World Bank fiduciary requirements and standards
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Use of IRC as implementing agency with proven capacity; intensive capacity building support to NGO sub-grantees through PEERS framework; development of exit and sustainability plans; introduction of 'graduation scheme' for NGO partners; hands-on implementation support from World Bank

identified_risks.risk_description: Government spending on social protection provides very little support for vulnerable populations (5.5% of GDP for only 2% of population); lack of comprehensive government approach to GBV, mental health, and disability sectors due to fiscal constraints and limited technical knowledge
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project design acknowledges need for broader public sector reform beyond project scope; partnership with government technical agencies (MoPH-NMHP, MoJ, MoSA) to align activities with national strategies; discussions with Lebanon Emergency Social Safety Net Project team on enhancing linkages with Social Development Centers

identified_risks.risk_category: Technical Design of Project or Program
identified_risks.risk_description: Limited extension of project scope; need for improved data collection and disaggregation; sustainability concerns for services beyond project closing
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Lessons learned from Phase I integrated into design; improved collection of disaggregated data; capacity building graduation scheme; exit and sustainability planning; public showcasing of successful activities and capacity progression; linking graduated sub-grantees with prospective donors

identified_risks.risk_description: Complex stakeholder environment with multiple actors; need for effective partnership across government, NGOs, UN agencies, bilateral donors, and private sector
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Extensive consultations conducted with diverse stakeholders; multi-stakeholder partnership approach; routine consultation and information sharing with PROSPECTS partners; participation in 10 programmatic working groups; hosting of Lebanese Humanitarian INGO Forum

identified_risks.risk_description: Deteriorating socio-economic, financial, and political situation; perception of fraud and corruption; lack of responsiveness of service providers due to currency fluctuation; sensitive nature of support; overlapping efforts between NGOs
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Advance planning; qualified PMU maintained with additional consultants if needed; close coordination with World Bank Task Team; synchronization of work between NGOs with regular communication; use of STEP system for tracking; PPSD to broaden social and employment benefits

identified_risks.risk_description: Potential GHG emissions from transportation support and generator usage for facility operations; extreme climate events (temperature variations, floods, landslides) could stress vulnerable beneficiaries and increase operating costs; poor quality of building infrastructure
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Climate risk assessed as low; support for transportation and generators justified based on urgent need and absence of alternatives; exploration of renewable energy integration (solar panels) if technically feasible and economically viable; maintenance support provisioned for facility upkeep; case-by-case coordination with operating partners for climate-related risks

triggered_safeguard_policies: ESS 1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
ESS 3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management
ESS 10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - IRC project grievance mechanism has multiple uptake channels and is functioning adequately. Grievances can be submitted to project-level mechanisms or World Bank's Grievance Redress Service (GRS). Communities can also submit complaints to World Bank's Accountability Mechanism (Inspection Panel or Dispute Resolution Service). No accidents, incidents, or SEA/SH grievances were reported under Phase I project
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Strong gender focus throughout project. Target groups include women survivors of GBV, accompanied girls, and at-risk boys. Economic empowerment and independence pilot for GBV beneficiaries includes market-based skills trainings for women and adolescent girls, financial literacy, business development support, and job counseling. Project recognizes gendered differences in coping mechanisms (poor female-headed households more likely to seek loans and rely on informal help, less likely to reduce food/education expenses but twice as likely to reduce health expenditures). Gender-based violence prevalence high (43% of women and 30% of men witnessed or knew someone who experienced violence). Project supports GBV Information Management System (GBVIMS) training and public sensitization campaigns targeting forcibly displaced persons and host communities. All activities apply intersectionality lens recognizing women with disabilities are 2-4 times more likely to experience GBV
vulnerable_groups: Survivors and individuals at-risk of gender-based violence (GBV)
Children under protective orders and at-risk children
Older persons (65+ years, representing 10% of population)
Persons with disabilities (estimated 10-15% of Lebanese population)
Forcibly displaced persons (FDPs) - particularly Syrian refugees (almost 90% living in extreme poverty)
Children at risk of early/child marriage
Persons with mental health challenges including those with suicidal ideation

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed for FCV context. Implements World Bank FCV strategy through direct partnerships with non-state actors to assist most vulnerable in protracted fragility. Recognizes that NGO and CSO sectors have been essential lifeline for vulnerable groups amid weak national systems. Project supports services that address immediate and longer-range social recovery needs. Includes public sensitization and awareness campaigns specifically targeting forcibly displaced persons and host communities to improve visibility of GBV issues, encourage positive collaboration between groups in face of tensions, and increase knowledge of available services. Project acknowledges intersecting crises and their differential impacts on vulnerable populations. Designed to bridge humanitarian-development nexus through lens of social vulnerability
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Lebanon classified as fragile state. Multiple concurrent crises since 2019: (1) Financial crisis - among top 10 most severe globally since mid-19th century, potentially top 3; (2) Port of Beirut explosion August 2020 causing loss of life, injury, displacement, physical damage; (3) COVID-19 pandemic; (4) Russia-Ukraine conflict spillover effects; (5) Regional tensions since October 2023 with limited escalation in Southern Lebanon. Economic contraction of 37.2% between 2018-2021, currency depreciation >90%, triple-digit inflation, balance of payments deficit, shrinking employment. Multidimensional poverty rate doubled from 40% to 80% between 2019-2022. Government spending on social protection minimal (5.5% of GDP for only 2% of population). Political deadlock and weak state capacity. Protracted crisis context with limited prospects for near-term stabilization
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Regional tensions since October 2023 have caused limited escalation of conflict in Southern Lebanon. Broader escalation of tensions and/or conflict events in region could rapidly and unpredictably augment national context. Project risk rated as substantial for 'Other' category due to security concerns. However, IRC is well-positioned to operate under emergency and FCV conditions with sufficient global and country capacity. Project activities include support for safe shelters for GBV survivors requiring confidentiality and security measures (e.g., preventing contractors from accessing sites for renewable energy installation due to protection of residents). Mobile Medical Units operate in fixed areas and may face risks from extreme weather or climate events. Project design acknowledges security constraints and operates accordingly
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - Forcibly displaced persons (FDPs) and migrants are cross-cutting beneficiary groups targeted across all project workstreams. Syrian refugees represent significant portion of vulnerable populations: almost 90% living in extreme poverty; 65% of children in shelter for boys under judicial protective orders are Syrian refugees; 32% of both Palestinian and Syrian refugees live with disabilities; 86% of recorded GBV incidents in first half 2023 were displaced Syrians (12% increase from 2022); 35% of callers to national mental health hotline are refugees; approximately 25% of older persons and persons with disabilities serviced are non-Lebanese. Phase II expands geographic coverage to Bekaa (refugee-dense area) and increases accessibility of services to FDPs and migrants through targeted outreach and awareness campaigns. Project recognizes refugee context feeds into child marriage dynamics where refugee girls and families may see it as pathway to opportunity/integration. Special focus on PHCCs servicing high shares of FDPs and migrants, with preliminary findings showing 51% of surveyed refugees access healthcare via PHCCs
